questions.question: Differentiate between LAN and WAN based on coverage area, speed, and ownership.
keyPoints: LAN covers small area (100m-2km), WAN covers large area (country/global)
LAN has high speed (100 Mbps-10 Gbps), WAN has lower speed (56 Kbps-100 Mbps)
LAN is privately owned, WAN uses public infrastructure like ISP networks

questions.question: What is a Metropolitan Area Network? Explain its characteristics and applications.
keyPoints: MAN covers a city or metropolitan region (10-100 km)
Uses fiber optic cables for high-speed connectivity
Connects multiple LANs within a city
Applications: cable TV networks, city-wide Wi-Fi, university campuses

questions.question: Explain Personal Area Network (PAN) with its technologies and limitations.
keyPoints: PAN is the smallest network type covering 10 meters
Uses Bluetooth, Infrared, and USB technologies
Connects personal devices like smartphones, tablets, wearables
Limitations: limited range, low speed, interference issues

questions.question: What are the advantages of Local Area Network?
keyPoints: High data transfer rates (100 Mbps to 10 Gbps)
Easy resource sharing (printers, files, internet)
Low cost of setup and maintenance
Easy to configure and manage
Provides centralized security and data backup

questions.question: Differentiate between PAN, LAN, MAN, and WAN based on geographical scope.
keyPoints: PAN: 10 meters (personal devices)
LAN: 100m to 2km (building/campus)
MAN: 10-100 km (city/metropolitan)
WAN: 100+ km (country/global)

questions.question: What is a Campus Area Network? How does it differ from a LAN?
keyPoints: CAN connects multiple buildings in a university or corporate campus (1-10 km)
LAN is confined to a single building
CAN uses multiple LANs connected together
CAN requires more complex infrastructure than single LAN

questions.question: Explain the concept of Home Area Network and list common technologies used.
keyPoints: HAN connects devices within a home environment
Common technologies: Wi-Fi, Ethernet, Zigbee, Z-Wave
Connects smart appliances, IoT devices, computers
Provides shared internet access and device control

questions.question: Define and explain all types of computer networks based on geographical scope. Also mention the typical range and data rates for each type.
keyPoints: PAN - Personal Area Network: 10m range, uses Bluetooth/IR, low speed
LAN - Local Area Network: 100m-2km, Ethernet/Wi-Fi, high speed (100 Mbps-10 Gbps)
CAN - Campus Area Network: 1-10km, connects multiple buildings
MAN - Metropolitan Area Network: 10-100km, fiber optic, 100 Mbps-10 Gbps
WAN - Wide Area Network: 100+km, uses routers/satellites, lower speeds

questions.question: Compare and contrast LAN and WAN in terms of coverage, speed, latency, cost, ownership, and technologies used.
keyPoints: Coverage: LAN (local/building), WAN (global/country)
Speed: LAN (high 100 Mbps-10 Gbps), WAN (lower 56 Kbps-100 Mbps)
Latency: LAN (low), WAN (high due to distance)
Cost: LAN (low), WAN (high setup and maintenance)
Ownership: LAN (private), WAN (public/ISP)
Technologies: LAN (Ethernet, Wi-Fi), WAN (routers, satellites, leased lines)

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Wide Area Network? Explain why organizations prefer WAN for inter-city communication.
keyPoints: Advantages of WAN: Global coverage, connects remote locations, enables internet access, shared resources across locations
Disadvantages: High latency, lower speed, expensive setup, complex management, security concerns

Organizations prefer WAN for inter-city communication due to: ability to connect geographically dispersed offices, centralized data management, communication through video conferencing, shared business applications

questions.question: Explain the hierarchical organization of networks. How do smaller networks connect to form larger networks? Discuss with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Networks form a hierarchy: PAN < LAN < CAN < MAN < WAN
PAN connects to LAN via Bluetooth pairing or USB
LAN connects to CAN via switches/routers within campus
CAN connects to MAN via fiber optic backbones
MAN connects to WAN via ISP connections and routers
Example: Home PAN connects to local ISP (WAN) through LAN router; University CAN connects to Internet backbone (WAN)
